narrative: As a Hispanic male applying to law school, you've shared your undergraduate GPA of 3.20, an LSAT score of 35, middle-family income background, and plans to attend full-time, along with your Year 1 and Year 3 school decile rankings both at the 2nd decile—meaning your grades placed you in the lower end of your class those years, compared to typical students who average around the 5th or 6th decile. The admissions model, which predicts first-time bar exam success based on profiles like yours, estimated a 56.8% chance that you would fail the bar exam, tipping the scales just over the threshold that led to your application's rejection. This prediction isn't a judgment on your potential or character, but a data-driven assessment drawing from patterns in thousands of similar students' outcomes. The most influential factor pushing toward this failure prediction was your Year 3 decile ranking, as being in the lower ranks there strongly signaled higher risk compared to peers who performed better in later years. Next in importance, your undergraduate GPA actually worked in your favor, pulling the prediction toward bar passage since your 3.20 is right around the average for students who succeed. Your Year 1 decile ranking had a smaller but still notable push toward failure, reinforcing concerns from that early lower performance. Overall, these academic deciles carried the heaviest weight because they often correlate strongly with bar exam readiness in the model's view of past students. While your LSAT was slightly below the typical 36.7 average and family income middle-of-the-road, they played lesser roles here. Compared to typical admitted students, who tend to have stronger mid-to-upper decile rankings, your profile aligned more closely with those who struggled on the bar. This doesn't define you—many in similar situations, including Hispanic students from middle-income families, have succeeded by targeting weak areas. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT for a boost, seeking academic advising to improve class rankings in any bridge programs, or applying to schools with holistic reviews that weigh your full story. Building a stronger academic trajectory now could shift future predictions in your favor.
